([Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language"): *Kombëtarja shqiptare e futbollit*) represents [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). It is governed by the [Albanian Football Federation](/wiki/Albanian_Football_Federation "Albanian Football Federation") (FSHF), the governing body for [football in Albania](/wiki/Football_in_Albania "Football in Albania"). It is a member of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") in Europe and [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in global competitions. The team's colours reference two national symbols: the [double-headed eagle](/wiki/Double-headed_eagle "Double-headed eagle") and the [country's bicolor](/wiki/Flag_of_Albania "Flag of Albania"). Their supporters are colloquially referred to as the *[Tifozat Kuq e Zi](/wiki/Tifozat_Kuq_e_Zi "Tifozat Kuq e Zi")* (Red and Black Fans).

The **Albania national football team** ([Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language"): *Kombëtarja shqiptare e futbollit*) represents [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") in men's international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). It is governed by the [Albanian Football Federation](/wiki/Albanian_Football_Federation "Albanian Football Federation") (FSHF), the governing body for [football in Albania](/wiki/Football_in_Albania "Football in Albania"). It is a member of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") in Europe and [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in global competitions. The team's colours reference two national symbols: the [double-headed eagle](/wiki/Double-headed_eagle "Double-headed eagle") and the [country's bicolor](/wiki/Flag_of_Albania "Flag of Albania"). Their supporters are colloquially referred to as the *[Tifozat Kuq e Zi](/wiki/Tifozat_Kuq_e_Zi "Tifozat Kuq e Zi")* (Red and Black Fans).

Since [its inception](/wiki/History_of_the_Albania_national_football_team "History of the Albania national football team") as a team, Albania first collected a major tournament trophy in [1946](/wiki/1946_Balkan_Cup "1946 Balkan Cup") with the [Balkan Cup](/wiki/Balkan_Cup "Balkan Cup"), followed by the [Malta International Tournament](/wiki/Malta_International_Football_Tournament "Malta International Football Tournament") ([2000](/wiki/2000_Malta_International_Football_Tournament "2000 Malta International Football Tournament")). At the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"), Albania has campaigned to qualify since their entry into FIFA in 1932. The team has reached the group stage of [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") twice ([2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016"), [2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024")). They have competed in the [UEFA Nations League](/wiki/UEFA_Nations_League "UEFA Nations League") since 2018.

Upon its admission into FIFA ranked 124th, Albania ascended to their peak [World Ranking](/wiki/FIFA_Men%27s_World_Ranking "FIFA Men's World Ranking") of 22nd in 2015. The country plays their home games in the [Arena Kombëtare](/wiki/Arena_Komb%C3%ABtare "Arena Kombëtare") in their capital city, [Tirana](/wiki/Tirana "Tirana").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Albania national football team](/wiki/History_of_the_Albania_national_football_team "History of the Albania national football team")

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Loro_Bori%C3%A7i_n%C3%AB_finalen_e_Ballkaniad%C3%ABs.jpg/250px-Loro_Bori%C3%A7i_n%C3%AB_finalen_e_Ballkaniad%C3%ABs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Loro_Bori%C3%A7i_n%C3%AB_finalen_e_Ballkaniad%C3%ABs.jpg)

[Loro Boriçi](/wiki/Loro_Bori%C3%A7i "Loro Boriçi") captained the national team in winning the [1946 Balkan Cup](/wiki/1946_Balkan_Cup "1946 Balkan Cup").

Although it never played any matches, the Albania national football team existed before the [Albanian Football Association](/wiki/Albanian_Football_Association "Albanian Football Association") (Federata Shqiptare e Futbollit; FSHF) was created on 6 June 1930. Albania joined [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") during a congress held between 12 and 16 June. Albania was invited to play in the 1934 World Cup, but did not take part due to logistical problems.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Albania played its first international match against [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia_national_football_team "Yugoslavia national football team") in 1946, which ended in a 3–2 home defeat at [Qemal Stafa Stadium](/wiki/Qemal_Stafa_Stadium "Qemal Stafa Stadium").[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1946, Albania also participated for the first time in the [Balkan Cup](/wiki/1946_Balkan_Cup "1946 Balkan Cup") in which Albania won by beating [Romania](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") 1–0 in the final.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 1954, Albania was one of the founding members of [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"). Albania waited until 1962 to compete for the first time in a UEFA Euro Cup competition, and the reason being Albania got past the first leg against Greece, for political reasons forfeited the game winning twice 0-3 and 3-0 and in Total 6–0 on Aggregate, to qualify so for the first and only time so far, for the Round of 16 in a UEFA competition.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Dizdari2007-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) At the end of the tournament Albania ranked 9th in Europe.[[11]](#cite_note-Armillotta@Panorama-11)[[12]](#cite_note-UEFA_website_on_Albania-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Albania participated at the [1964 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics") in the [Men's qualification](/wiki/Football_at_the_1964_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_qualification "Football at the 1964 Summer Olympics – Men's qualification") tournament, and closely lost both matches against [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_national_football_team "Bulgaria national football team") in the preliminary round.[[14]](#cite_note-14) They would participate for the second and last time at the [1972 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics") in which they faced [Romania](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") at the [European qualifiers](/wiki/Football_at_the_1972_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_European_Qualifiers "Football at the 1972 Summer Olympics – Men's European Qualifiers") in the playoffs. Albania would lose both matches narrowly, 2–1 in the first leg and at home in the second leg.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Pull%C3%AB_e_Kampionatit_Europian_t%C3%AB_Futbollit_%281984%29.png/250px-Pull%C3%AB_e_Kampionatit_Europian_t%C3%AB_Futbollit_%281984%29.png)](/wiki/File:Pull%C3%AB_e_Kampionatit_Europian_t%C3%AB_Futbollit_(1984).png)

Albanian stamp honouring [Euro 1984](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1984 "UEFA Euro 1984").

Albania participated for the first time in a qualifying phase of a [World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") in [1966](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"). The team was drawn in the [Group 5](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA_%E2%80%93_Group_5) "1966 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA – Group 5)"), finishing in the last position with only one point from six matches. In the [qualifiers](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1968_qualifying "UEFA Euro 1968 qualifying") of [UEFA Euro 1968](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1968 "UEFA Euro 1968"), Albania drew 0–0 against [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany_national_football_team "West Germany national football team"), denying the Germans a chance to participate in the finals.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Albania did not participate in [World Cup 1970](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup "1970 FIFA World Cup"), [UEFA Euro 1976](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1976 "UEFA Euro 1976"), [World Cup 1978](/wiki/1978_FIFA_World_Cup "1978 FIFA World Cup") and [UEFA Euro 1980](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_1980 "UEFA Euro 1980") qualifying for unknown political reasons.

After six years without playing any international matches, Albania entered the [1982 World Cup](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup "1982 FIFA World Cup"), managing to finish second-to-last in the qualifying stages. For qualifying for the World Cup four years later, Albania beat [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team") in [Tirana](/wiki/Tirana "Tirana") 2–0 as well as drawing [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team") in [Mielec](/wiki/Mielec "Mielec") 2–2.

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In the 2002 World Cup [qualifiers](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_9 "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 9"), Albania only beat [Greece](/wiki/Greece_national_football_team "Greece national football team"). [For the Euros two years later](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2004_qualifying_Group_10 "UEFA Euro 2004 qualifying Group 10"), Albania beat [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_football_team "Russia national football team") 3–1 at [Loro Boriçi Stadium](/wiki/Loro_Bori%C3%A7i_Stadium "Loro Boriçi Stadium").[[19]](#cite_note-First_match-19) This match was also the debut of the German coach [Hans-Peter Briegel](/wiki/Hans-Peter_Briegel "Hans-Peter Briegel") who led Albania to an undefeated run in home matches. Despite good results, Albania finished in the penultimate spot with only eight points. Albania defeated Greece 2–1 two months after they won [Euro 2004](/wiki/Euro_2004 "Euro 2004").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In [Euro 2008 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying_Group_G "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying Group G"), Albania defeated [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_national_football_team "Luxembourg national football team") twice, and also drew 2–2 with [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_national_football_team "Belarus national football team") and 0–0 away with [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria_national_football_team "Bulgaria national football team"). Albania's Euro campaign ended with a 6–1 loss away to [Romania](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") which resulted in the resignation of coach [Otto Barić](/wiki/Otto_Bari%C4%87 "Otto Barić") and his assistant.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In December 2007, [Arie Haan](/wiki/Arie_Haan "Arie Haan") was named Albania's head coach by signing a two-year contract for the [2010 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_1 "2010 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group 1"). Albania won only one match, with seven points from ten matches and Haan was replaced by [Josip Kuže](/wiki/Josip_Ku%C5%BEe "Josip Kuže") in May 2009 following the end of the campaign.[[22]](#cite_note-22) However, Kuže failed to lead Albania to better results as the team ended the [Euro 2012 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_qualifying_Group_D "UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying Group D") with nine points from ten matches. With Kuže in charge, Albania recorded its second biggest win in history by defeating [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus_national_football_team "Cyprus national football team") 6–1 at home.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Josip Kuže parted ways with Albania after three and a half years, and in December 2011, Italian coach [Gianni de Biasi](/wiki/Gianni_de_Biasi "Gianni de Biasi") replaced him.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Albania was, at one point, second in the [2014 World Cup qualifying](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification") group, but then lost to [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team") and [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland_national_football_team "Iceland national football team"), as well as at home against [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team"), and drawing in Cyprus.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/AUT_vs._ALB_2016-03-26_%28004%29.jpg/330px-AUT_vs._ALB_2016-03-26_%28004%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:AUT_vs._ALB_2016-03-26_(004).jpg)

Albania squad wearing the visitors' uniform, 2016.

Albania began [UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying Group I](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying_Group_I "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying Group I") with an emphatic 1–0 away win against [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team"),[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) followed by a 1–1 draw against [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_national_football_team "Denmark national football team") at the newly renovated [Elbasan Arena](/wiki/Elbasan_Arena "Elbasan Arena").[[28]](#cite_note-28) They then beat Serbia 3–0 in [a match full of incidents](/wiki/Serbia_v_Albania_(UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying) "Serbia v Albania (UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying)"). They finally qualified for a [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") after beating [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia_national_football_team "Armenia national football team") in Yerevan 0–3, its first appearance in the country's history at a major men's football tournament.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In the tournament itself, Albania lost 1–0 to [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team") to an early header in the 5th Minute from [Fabian Schär](/wiki/Fabian_Sch%C3%A4r "Fabian Schär") despite being one man down to a Red Card for [Lorik Cana](/wiki/Lorik_Cana "Lorik Cana") Albania held its ground as they came close to an equalizer as [Shkëlzen Gashi](/wiki/Shk%C3%ABlzen_Gashi "Shkëlzen Gashi") as he missed an sitter in front of goalie [Yann Sommer](/wiki/Yann_Sommer "Yann Sommer").[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) In the following match they lost 2–0 to the hosts [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") despite an close fought match being able to defend against the favourites until the last minutes conceding two goals from [Dimitri Payet](/wiki/Dimitri_Payet "Dimitri Payet") and [Griezmann](/wiki/Antoine_Griezmann "Antoine Griezmann").[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) While they beat in the third Group match Romania 1–0 by a header scored from [Armando Sadiku](/wiki/Armando_Sadiku "Armando Sadiku") before halftime, securing Albania's first ever win in a [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship").[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36) The team finished last among the third-placed teams and were eliminated in the group stage.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

In [2018 World Cup qualification](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)"), Albania was drawn in a [group](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_G "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group G") with [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team"), [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein_national_football_team "Liechtenstein national football team"), [Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_national_football_team "Macedonia national football team"), and [Israel](/wiki/Israel_national_football_team "Israel national football team"). Despite the difficult draw, they placed third in the group, their best result in history without qualifying for the final tournament. During this time, manager [Gianni De Biasi](/wiki/Gianni_De_Biasi "Gianni De Biasi") resigned after disputes with the FSHF and was replaced by [Christian Panucci](/wiki/Christian_Panucci "Christian Panucci").[[37]](#cite_note-38) Albania played poorly in the [2018–19 UEFA Nations League](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_UEFA_Nations_League "2018–19 UEFA Nations League"), winning only once against Israel and losing the return leg as well as both matches against [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team").[[38]](#cite_note-39) Panucci would be sacked after a 2–0 defeat to [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team") in the opening match of [UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2020 qualifying").[[39]](#cite_note-40) [Edoardo Reja](/wiki/Edoardo_Reja "Edoardo Reja") replaced Panucci as Albania faced home and away series against both [2018 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup") champions [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") and [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland_national_football_team "Iceland national football team") as well as the return leg against [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team").[[40]](#cite_note-41) In the end, Albania placed fourth in the group, failing to qualify.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

After their close miss to qualify for the [2022 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_I "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group I") in Qatar, in which Albania finished third in the group,[[42]](#cite_note-43) in the [UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying Group E](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024_qualifying_Group_E "UEFA Euro 2024 qualifying Group E") Albania were drawn together with [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_national_football_team "Czech Republic national football team"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team"), [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands_national_football_team "Faroe Islands national football team") and [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova_national_football_team "Moldova national football team").[[43]](#cite_note-44) In the following matches Albania was undefeated, and secured the qualification for the second time in history for the [UEFA European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") at the [UEFA Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024").[[44]](#cite_note-45)

At the [UEFA Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024").[[45]](#cite_note-46) They were drawn into a "[group of death](/wiki/Group_of_death "Group of death")" against [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team"), and [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_national_football_team "Croatia national football team"), all three opponents being Euros and World Cup medalists. With two losses to Spain and Italy and a draw against Croatia, the team finished at the bottom of their group and did not advance.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

Albania is set to compete in the [UEFA Nations League B](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_UEFA_Nations_League_B "2024–25 UEFA Nations League B") in fall 2024, followed by [qualification](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)") matches for the [2026 World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup").

## Team image

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Team image")]

### Nicknames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Nicknames")]

The team's nickname is commonly known as "*Kuqezinjtë*" ("The Red and Blacks") During the period before 2016, the national teams of Albania and Kosovo exchanged players with each other due to the complexities of descent brought on by Kosovo's independence, leading supporters to refer to each team as the other team's "reserve" side.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### Kits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Kits")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4a/Commons-logo.svg/40px-Commons-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Commons-logo.svg)

Wikimedia Commons has media related to [Albania national football team kits](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Albania_national_football_team_kits "commons:Albania national football team kits").

Albania's uniform colors are red and black, mirroring the [nation's flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Albania "Flag of Albania"). The team typically wears red shirts, black shorts and red socks. Away kits are usually all-white, with red and black trimming.

Over the years, the national team has sported kit wear from renowned sportswear brands such as [Legea](/wiki/Legea "Legea"), [Puma](/wiki/Puma_(brand) "Puma (brand)"), [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") and [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas"), to name a few. The partnership with Puma began in 1996 and lasted nine years. Nike outfitted the team during the [UEFA Euro 2008](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2008 qualifying") and the [2010 World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "2010 FIFA World Cup qualification"). A collaboration with Adidas followed, in a contract signing which allowed it to manufacture kits for the [Euro 2012 qualifiers](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2012 qualifying"), as well as the [2014 World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification") and the [Euro 2016 qualifiers](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 24 March 2016, the [Albanian Football Federation](/wiki/Albanian_Football_Federation "Albanian Football Federation") unveiled a New Jersey for the national team players, set to be worn during the forthcoming [UEFA Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") tournament, hosted that year in France. Manufactured by [Macron](/wiki/Macron_(sportswear) "Macron (sportswear)"), the jersey redesign, labelled "Triumfi," was conceptualised by Tirana-based studio PIK Creative.[[48]](#cite_note-FirstSix-49)[[49]](#cite_note-SecondSix-50)

### Home stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Home stadium")]

Main articles: [Arena Kombëtare](/wiki/Arena_Komb%C3%ABtare "Arena Kombëtare"), [Elbasan Arena](/wiki/Elbasan_Arena "Elbasan Arena"), and [Loro Boriçi Stadium](/wiki/Loro_Bori%C3%A7i_Stadium "Loro Boriçi Stadium")

See also: [Qemal Stafa Stadium](/wiki/Qemal_Stafa_Stadium "Qemal Stafa Stadium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Air_Albania_Stadium_2024.jpg/250px-Air_Albania_Stadium_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Air_Albania_Stadium_2024.jpg)

[Arena Kombëtare](/wiki/Arena_Komb%C3%ABtare "Arena Kombëtare") is the home ground of the national team.

For most of Albania's history, the home stadium was [Qemal Stafa Stadium](/wiki/Qemal_Stafa_Stadium "Qemal Stafa Stadium") in [Tirana](/wiki/Tirana "Tirana"). Construction started in April 1939 during the [Italy regime](/wiki/Italian_protectorate_of_Albania_(1939%E2%80%931943) "Italian protectorate of Albania (1939–1943)"), lasted for three years but stopped briefly in August 1943 following the [fall of fascist regime](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy").[[50]](#cite_note-History-51) The stadium had an [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Olympic_Stadium "Olympic Stadium") shape, as idealised by [Gherardo Bosio](/wiki/Gherardo_Bosio "Gherardo Bosio"), a young fascist architect from [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence").[[51]](#cite_note-Qemal_Stafa_Stadium-52) The stadium's initial capacity was 15,000, due to the fact that Tirana at that time had only 60,000 inhabitants.[[51]](#cite_note-Qemal_Stafa_Stadium-52) It was named after [Qemal Stafa](/wiki/Qemal_Stafa "Qemal Stafa"), a [Hero of Albania](/wiki/Hero_of_Albania "Hero of Albania") in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[52]](#cite_note-53) The stadium was inaugurated officially on 7 October 1946 when Albania played its first competitive match against Yugoslavia.[[53]](#cite_note-Stadium1-54) Ever since then, a further 130 international matches took place in the stadium, with the last being a friendly against Georgia in November 2015.[[54]](#cite_note-55) In 2005, Cecilia de Marco and Elisabetta Lorusso, two young Italian students, called the stadium as "one of the strongest symbols of Italian impact in Albania".[[50]](#cite_note-History-51) In November 2013, Qemal Stafa Stadium was shut down by FIFA for not fulfilling international standards.[[55]](#cite_note-56) The stadium's demolition started in June 2016, and it was announced that it would be replaced by [Arena Kombëtare](/wiki/Arena_Komb%C3%ABtare "Arena Kombëtare").[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Albania has played home matches at other venues; outside Tirana, the national team has played matches in [Flamurtari Stadium](/wiki/Flamurtari_Stadium "Flamurtari Stadium") in [Vlorë](/wiki/Vlor%C3%AB "Vlorë"),[[57]](#cite_note-Stadium6-58) [Tomori Stadium](/wiki/Tomori_Stadium "Tomori Stadium") in [Berat](/wiki/Berat "Berat"),[[58]](#cite_note-Stadium5-59) [Niko Dovana Stadium](/wiki/Niko_Dovana_Stadium "Niko Dovana Stadium") in [Durrës](/wiki/Durr%C3%ABs "Durrës"),[[59]](#cite_note-Stadium4-60) [Loro Boriçi Stadium](/wiki/Loro_Bori%C3%A7i_Stadium "Loro Boriçi Stadium") in [Shkodër](/wiki/Shkod%C3%ABr "Shkodër")[[60]](#cite_note-Stadium3-61) and [Elbasan Arena](/wiki/Elbasan_Arena "Elbasan Arena") in [Elbasan](/wiki/Elbasan "Elbasan").[[61]](#cite_note-Stadium2-62) In February 2014, renovations for Elbasan Arena (at the time Ruzhdi Bizhuta Stadium) started.[[62]](#cite_note-63) The work lasted for seven months, and the stadium was inaugurated on 9 October when Albania played a friendly match against [KF Elbasani](/wiki/KF_Elbasani "KF Elbasani")'s under-19 squad; Albania won the match 17–0.[[63]](#cite_note-64) The official inauguration occurred two days later in the [Euro 2016 qualifying](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying "UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying") match against [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_national_football_team "Denmark national football team").[[64]](#cite_note-65)

The first international match at Loro Boriçi Stadium was played on 29 March 2003 against [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_football_team "Russia national football team"), with Albania winning 3–1.[[19]](#cite_note-First_match-19) In October 2014, [Prime Minister of Albania](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Albania "Prime Minister of Albania"), [Edi Rama](/wiki/Edi_Rama "Edi Rama"), promised the reconstruction of the stadium.[[65]](#cite_note-66) The stadium's construction started in May 2015 and finished in August 2016.[[66]](#cite_note-67) In 2016–17, the stadium served temporary as the home of [Kosovo national team](/wiki/Kosovo_national_football_team "Kosovo national football team"), due to stadiums in [Mitrovica](/wiki/Mitrovica,_Kosovo "Mitrovica, Kosovo") and [Pristina](/wiki/Pristina "Pristina") being renovated and not meeting UEFA standards.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

#### Kosovo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Kosovo")]

Main article: [Brotherly derby](/wiki/Brotherly_derby "Brotherly derby")

This derby is otherwise known as Brotherly derby ([Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language"): *Derbi vëllazëror*), or the Brotherly ([Albanian](/wiki/Albanian_language "Albanian language"): *Vëllazërorja*). The documented beginnings of this derby date back to World War II, specifically on 29 November 1942, where they played a [friendly match](/wiki/Exhibition_game#Association_football "Exhibition game") as part of the celebrations for the 30th anniversary of the independence of Albania. The match ended with a 2–0 win for Tirana.[[a]](#cite_note-70)[[69]](#cite_note-71)[[70]](#cite_note-Besnik_Dizdari_Panorama_Sport-72)

#### Serbia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Serbia")]

Main article: [Albania–Serbia football rivalry](/wiki/Albania%E2%80%93Serbia_football_rivalry "Albania–Serbia football rivalry")

This football rivalry between Albania and Serbia is mainly due to [historical tensions](/wiki/Albania%E2%80%93Serbia_relations "Albania–Serbia relations") between the two countries. It has been described as "one of the fiercest rivalries in the world". During the [Euro 2016 qualifying phase](/wiki/Serbia_v_Albania_(UEFA_Euro_2016_qualifying) "Serbia v Albania (UEFA Euro 2016 qualifying)"), with Portugal, Denmark and Armenia, the Serbian crowd were chanting "kill, kill the Albanians"[[71]](#cite_note-73) and were throwing flares at the Albanian players, after which there was a clash between the two teams.[[72]](#cite_note-74)

Both countries were drawn in the same group for the [2026 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_K "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group K"), with England, Latvia and Andorra.

### Supporters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Albania_national_football_team&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Supporters")]

Main article: [Tifozat Kuq e Zi](/wiki/Tifozat_Kuq_e_Zi "Tifozat Kuq e Zi")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Tifozat_Kuq_e_Zi_ne_Elbasan_Arena.jpg/330px-Tifozat_Kuq_e_Zi_ne_Elbasan_Arena.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tifozat_Kuq_e_Zi_ne_Elbasan_Arena.jpg)

A choreography of Tifozat Kuq e Zi in the national team's first match at [Elbasan Arena](/wiki/Elbasan_Arena "Elbasan Arena") against Denmark in October 2014

**Tifozat Kuq e Zi** (Red and Black Fans) is a non-profit [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") supporters' association for the Albania national football team and various national team sportive activities.[[73]](#cite_note-TKZ-75)[[74]](#cite_note-76) It was founded on 25 December 2003.[[73]](#cite_note-TKZ-75) In cooperation with [FSHF](/wiki/Albanian_Football_Federation "Albanian Football Federation"), it organises trips for football fans to visit games, and develops and sells merchandise to support itself and fund sporting related projects.

Tifozat Kuq e Zi stands firm in the political view that [Albanians](/wiki/Albanian_people "Albanian people") should share only one national team and have continuous aspirations to join in one state (Një Komb, Një Kombëtare), i.e. unification of Albania, Kosovo, etc.[[75]](#cite_note-77)[[76]](#cite_note-78) In this sense, TKZ is joined by different supporters' associations throughout Albanian-speaking regions mainly in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") (Shqiponjat of [Peja](/wiki/Peja_(Kosovo) "Peja (Kosovo)"), [Kuqezinjet e Jakoves](/wiki/KF_V%C3%ABllaznimi "KF Vëllaznimi") of [Gjakova](/wiki/Gjakova "Gjakova"), [Plisat](/wiki/FC_Prishtina "FC Prishtina") of [Prishtina](/wiki/Pristina "Pristina"), [Torcida](/wiki/Torcida_Mitrovic%C3%AB "Torcida Mitrovicë") of [Mitrovica](/wiki/Kosovska_Mitrovica "Kosovska Mitrovica"), etc.), [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia") ([Ballistët](/wiki/FK_Shk%C3%ABndija#Supporters "FK Shkëndija") of [Tetovë](/wiki/Tetovo "Tetovo"), [Ilirët](/wiki/FK_Bashkimi_(2011) "FK Bashkimi (2011)") of [Kumanovë](/wiki/Kumanovo "Kumanovo"), [Shvercerat](/wiki/FK_Shkupi#Supporters "FK Shkupi") of [Shkupi](/wiki/Skopje "Skopje"), etc.) and in Albania itself ([Ultras Guerrils](/wiki/FK_Partizani_Tirana#Ultras_Guerrils "FK Partizani Tirana") of [Partizani Tirana](/wiki/FK_Partizani_Tirana "FK Partizani Tirana"), [Tirona Fanatics](/wiki/Tirona_Fanatics "Tirona Fanatics") of [KF Tirana](/wiki/KF_Tirana "KF Tirana"), [Vllaznit Ultras](/wiki/KF_Vllaznia_Shkod%C3%ABr#Fans "KF Vllaznia Shkodër") of [Vllaznia Shkodër](/wiki/KF_Vllaznia_Shkod%C3%ABr "KF Vllaznia Shkodër"), [Ujqërit e Deborës](/wiki/KF_Sk%C3%ABnderbeu_Kor%C3%A7%C3%AB#Supporters "KF Skënderbeu Korçë") of [Skënderbeu Korçë](/wiki/KF_Sk%C3%ABnderbeu_Kor%C3%A7%C3%AB "KF Skënderbeu Korçë"), Shqiponjat of [KF Besa Pejë](/wiki/KF_Besa_Pej%C3%AB "KF Besa Pejë") and many other different Albanian fans).[[73]](#cite_note-TKZ-75)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Tifosi_albanesi_stadio_Palermo.jpg/250px-Tifosi_albanesi_stadio_Palermo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tifosi_albanesi_stadio_Palermo.jpg)

[Albanian](/wiki/Albanians "Albanians") and [Arbëreshë](/wiki/Arb%C3%ABresh%C3%AB_people "Arbëreshë people") fans in [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"), for the Italy-Albania [2018 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup") [qualifier match](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_UEFA_Group_G "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – UEFA Group G") on 24 March 2017

The ongoing dispute between the Ministry of Culture, Youth and Sports and the Football Federation of Albania has been seen as a political intrusion by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") and [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"), which led to the banning of Albania from international sportive activities. FSHF president [Armand Duka](/wiki/Armand_Duka "Armand Duka") is highly unwanted by the TKZ who have numerously asked for his resignation believing he is responsible for internal corruption in the [Albanian Football Federation](/wiki/Albanian_Football_Federation "Albanian Football Federation").[[77]](#cite_note-79)[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81)[[80]](#cite_note-82)

The TKZ have been praised by many different football players and managers, whom were not just Albanian. One example is with Switzerland's former coach, [Ottmar Hitzfeld](/wiki/Ottmar_Hitzfeld "Ottmar Hitzfeld"), who was astonished by how many Albanian fans turned up and how enthusiastic they were in [2014 FIFA World Cup qualifying](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(UEFA) "2014 FIFA World Cup qualification (UEFA)") match between [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_national_football_team "Switzerland national football team") and Albania where the Swiss won 2–0 thanks to goals from [Gökhan Inler](/wiki/G%C3%B6khan_Inler "Gökhan Inler") and Kosovo-born [Xherdan Shaqiri](/wiki/Xherdan_Shaqiri "Xherdan Shaqiri"). He did not believe that there were 12,000 Albanian fans in the stands which was more than how many Swiss fans turned up for the game. He stated that "Albanian fans are fantastic and the most passionate fans I have ever seen".[[81]](#cite_note-83) During that campaign, TKZ attended all games Albania played apart from a match against [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus_national_football_team "Cyprus national football team") in [Nicosia](/wiki/Nicosia "Nicosia") and were also large in numbers in the away games to [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team") in [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor") and [Norway](/wiki/Norway_national_football_team "Norway national football team") in Oslo.[[82]](#cite_note-84)[[83]](#cite_note-85)[[84]](#cite_note-86)
